benchmark_metadata.methodology: Scientific statistical analysis with 5 sample runs per command

winner.verdict: ✅ DECENT - Best efficiency with zero MCP dependencies

loser.verdict: 💀 DUMPSTER FIRE - Maximum capability at maximum cost

key_findings: MCP dependencies create exponential performance costs
Simple commands dramatically outperform complex ones
Success rate inversely correlates with efficiency
Token consumption varies by 10x across commands

ultrathink-pure-xml.savage_analysis: The only command that doesn't embarrass itself. Zero MCP dependencies means it actually responds in human timescales. 86% success rate proves you don't need to orchestrate a symphony to make toast.

adhd-evening-reflect.savage_analysis: Fastest response time at 2.96s proves that data processing doesn't require a PhD in complexity theory. Single MCP dependency keeps it sane while still being useful.

java-rapid-implementation.savage_analysis: Claims to be 'rapid' but takes 6.18s. That's like calling a turtle 'swift' - technically it moves, but you'll be waiting. At least it succeeds 90% of the time when it bothers to finish.

senior-developer-analysis.savage_analysis: Burns 5,948 tokens to tell you what you already know. Like hiring a consultant to explain why water is wet. High success rate doesn't justify the token hemorrhaging.

java-test-driven-development.savage_analysis: 460 lines of command for TDD. By the time it finishes explaining RED-GREEN-REFACTOR, you could have written the code, tests, and taken a coffee break. 7.63s response time for 'rapid' development methodology.

java-clean-code-generator.savage_analysis: 819 lines of pseudo-philosophical Java wisdom that burns 14,907 tokens per invocation. Takes 10.16s to generate 'clean' code - ironic, since this command is the opposite of clean. Yes, 93.5% success rate, but at what cost? Your sanity and token budget.

ultrathink-hybrid-mcp.savage_analysis: 15.61s response time with 81.9% success rate. You'd have better odds at a casino. Three MCP dependencies create a house of cards that collapses 18% of the time while burning tokens like a bonfire.

ultrathink-full-mcp.savage_analysis: The crown jewel of over-engineering. Burns 20,326 tokens and 15.98s to achieve 'maximum capability' - like using a nuclear reactor to power a nightlight. Sure, 96.9% success rate, but you could buy a small server with the computational cost.

cost_analysis.cost_efficiency_insight: Simple commands are 10x more token-efficient than complex ones

performance_analysis.performance_insight: MCP dependencies are response time poison

reliability_analysis.reliability_insight: Complexity improves success rate but destroys efficiency

immediate_actions: Deprecate ultrathink-full-mcp unless solving actual complex problems
Promote ultrathink-pure-xml for 90% of use cases
Add MCP dependency warnings to command documentation
Implement automatic complexity routing to prevent overkill
architectural_insights: Token consumption scales exponentially with complexity
Success rate has inverse relationship with efficiency
Thinking blocks beyond 10 show diminishing returns
design_principles: Default to simple, escalate when necessary
MCP dependencies must justify their performance cost
Complexity is a last resort, not a first choice

savage_conclusions.winner_analysis: ultrathink-pure-xml wins by being boring and effective. Sometimes the tortoise beats the hare because the hare tripped over its own complexity.
savage_conclusions.loser_analysis: ultrathink-full-mcp is a monument to over-engineering. It's like bringing a aircraft carrier to a street race - technically impressive, completely impractical.
savage_conclusions.methodology_validation: This scientific approach reveals what users feel intuitively: simple tools work better than complex ones. The data doesn't lie, even when our egos want it to.
savage_conclusions.final_verdict: Your command suite spans from 'actually usable' to 'academic curiosity.' The best commands solve problems efficiently. The worst commands solve efficiency problems.
